title: The seven primitive types
instruction:
JavaScript has seven primitive types. You have already seen a few — now let's meet them all.

Run this. Notice how `typeof` reports each one.
starter:
console.log(typeof "hello");      // string
console.log(typeof 42);           // number
console.log(typeof 3.14);         // number (same type!)
console.log(typeof true);         // boolean
console.log(typeof undefined);    // undefined
console.log(typeof null);         // object (famous bug)
console.log(typeof Symbol());     // symbol
console.log(typeof 9007199254740993n); // bigint
successMessage: JavaScript has ONE number type for both integers and decimals — unlike Python. And typeof null === 'object' is a bug from 1995 that cannot be fixed. You now know the whole type system.

title: number — JavaScript does floats by default
instruction:
JavaScript uses a 64-bit float for ALL numbers — integers and decimals share one type.

This creates a famous quirk:

```js
0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3  // false!
```

Run this, then check why `0.1 + 0.2` doesn't equal `0.3` and how to work around it.
successMessage: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004 — floating-point math! Every language with 64-bit floats has this. For money: always use .toFixed(2) or store amounts in cents (integers).

title: string — text in JavaScript
instruction:
Strings can use single quotes, double quotes, or backticks. Backtick strings (template literals) are the most powerful — they support multi-line and embedded expressions.

Create three strings using each quote style, then combine them.
successMessage: Template literals (backticks) are modern JS's preferred string style — they support interpolation, multi-line text, and expressions. Use them by default.

title: boolean — just two values
instruction:
`true` and `false`. That's it. They control every `if` statement, every loop condition, every decision.

JavaScript also has comparison operators that PRODUCE booleans:

- `===` strict equality (type + value)
- `!==` strict not-equal
- `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`

Run these and confirm the outputs.
successMessage: 5 === "5" is false because === checks type too. This is strict equality — the right default. Later you'll meet == (loose equality) and understand why most style guides ban it.

title: null vs undefined — two 'nothing' values
instruction:
JavaScript has two ways to say 'no value here', which confuses everyone:

- `undefined` — the value was never set. JavaScript assigns this automatically.
- `null` — the value was intentionally set to nothing. A deliberate empty.

Rule of thumb: JavaScript gives you `undefined`. You give things `null` when you want to clear them.
successMessage: undefined = JS didn't set it. null = you cleared it. Use null intentionally — don't set things to undefined yourself. That's JavaScript's job.

title: The coercion trap — == vs ===
instruction:
This is one of JavaScript's most famous footguns. `==` (loose equality) converts types before comparing — `===` (strict equality) does not.

Run this and study every result. Then remember: **always use `===`**.
starter:
// Loose == coerces types before comparing:
console.log(0 == false);      // true
console.log(1 == true);       // true
console.log("" == false);     // true
console.log(null == undefined);// true
console.log("5" == 5);        // true

console.log("--- strict ===");

// Strict === never coerces:
console.log(0 === false);     // false
console.log("5" === 5);       // false
console.log(null === undefined);// false
successMessage: == hides type mismatches behind silent conversions. Every major JavaScript style guide (Airbnb, Google, Standard) requires ===. Treat == as if it doesn't exist.

title: Type conversion — be explicit
instruction:
Rather than letting JS coerce types silently, convert explicitly:

- `Number("42")` → 42
- `String(42)` → `"42"`
- `Boolean(0)` → `false`
- `parseInt("42px")` → 42 (stops at non-number)

Fix the broken additions by converting explicitly.
hint: Number("10") to convert string to number.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: "5" + 3 = "53" not 8 — that is the coercion trap! Number() is explicit and safe. Always convert intentionally instead of letting JavaScript guess.

title: Truthy and falsy
instruction:
JavaScript treats certain values as `false` in boolean contexts:

| Falsy | Truthy |
|-------|--------|
| `false`, `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN` | Everything else |

This lets you write `if (name)` instead of `if (name !== "")` — but it also creates traps. `if (0)` won't run even though 0 is a valid number.

Run and study each.
successMessage: Six falsy values only — everything else is truthy. Note: empty array [] and empty object {} are both TRUTHY! That surprises many people.

title: NaN — the weird number
instruction:
`NaN` means 'Not a Number' — but `typeof NaN === 'number'`. It is JavaScript's way of saying 'I tried to do math but the result is nonsensical'.

`NaN` is the only value in JavaScript that is not equal to itself:

```js
NaN === NaN  // false!
```

Use `Number.isNaN()` to check for it.
starter:
console.log(typeof NaN);           // 'number' — weird
console.log(0 / 0);                // NaN
console.log(parseInt("hello"));    // NaN

// NaN !== NaN is the only case of a value not equal to itself!
console.log(NaN === NaN);          // false!
console.log(NaN == NaN);           // false!

// The right way to check:
console.log(Number.isNaN(NaN));    // true
console.log(Number.isNaN(42));     // false
successMessage: NaN !== NaN — the only value in JavaScript not equal to itself. Always use Number.isNaN() to check for it. isNaN() (without Number.) has surprising coercion behaviour, so always use the Number. version.

title: All types in one program
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Create one variable of each type:
- A string (your name)
- A number (your age)
- A boolean (are you enjoying this?)
- `null` (a result not ready yet)
- `undefined` (declare it without assigning)

Print each with its type using typeof. Then show the famous bugs: `typeof null` and `NaN === NaN`.
hint: Use typeof to check each variable's type.

codeChecks.message: Use typeof to check each type.

successMessage: All seven primitive types covered — plus the two bugs (typeof null and NaN) that every JavaScript developer eventually discovers. Now you found them first. Chapter 3 done!
